## Goal
Short-TTL Ed25519 capability tokens allow members to perform offline operations (work:close, work:claim, etc.) without a convention server round-trip.

## Done condition
- pkg/captoken/ exists: token format, Sign(), Verify() (~60 LOC)
- Token struct: {subject, campfire, role, operations[], issued_at, expires_at, binding_msg_id}
- Signed by convention server's private key (the one named in the server-binding)
- Verify checks: signature against bound server pubkey, expires_at > now, operation in token.operations, subject matches sender
- TTLs: bulk safe ops (work:create/claim/update) = 48h; consequential (work:close/delegate) = 24h; high-stakes (work:gate-resolve) = 4h
- Test: token signed by server key, verified offline against binding pubkey → accepted
- Test: expired token → rejected
- Test: wrong operation in token → rejected
- Test: mismatched subject → rejected

## Files (§10 Wave 2)
- New: pkg/captoken/ — ~60 LOC

## Design reference
Design campfire: 36284e31fe77a3ecea91162d7c8826adc74b7dd1296f89ae69db68fab48a71cc. See §3.5 Offline Authorization: Capability Tokens. Revocation during TTL is intentional trade-off: token-TTL latency is documented. Reconciliation on reconnect: server reads log, posts retroactive fulfills messages.

## Goal
Project campfires use E2E encryption by default. Org observers read a shadow summary campfire maintained by the convention server, not the main campfire.

## Done condition
- rd init sets encrypted=true on the project campfire
- A second summary campfire is created by rd init; its ID written to .cf/config.toml [ready] summary_campfire
- campfire:summary-bind convention declaration: declares which summary campfire receives projections for a given project campfire
- Convention server: on every work:item-* operation, writes a work:item-summary to the bound summary campfire (title, status, priority, assignee, eta, updated_at — no body/comments)
- rd admit --role org-observer admits the key to the summary campfire, not the main campfire
- Test: org-observer running rd list reads only from summary campfire; main campfire content is not visible
- Test: work:item-summary is written on work:close, work:create, work:claim

- pkg/conventionserver/ — subscribe to main campfire stream, write summaries

Design campfire: 36284e31fe77a3ecea91162d7c8826adc74b7dd1296f89ae69db68fab48a71cc. See §6.2 E2E encryption + observer. IMPORTANT: The convention server is a TRUSTED INTERMEDIARY — it holds a full-member key to every E2E campfire it serves. This trust model MUST be documented in user-facing output.

payload.context:
## Goal
Three new commands handle the full invite workflow: join a project by name, admit a requester, revoke a member.

## Done condition
- rd join <name> resolves the name, attempts to join, posts work:join-request if consult policy bounces it, blocks/polls for admission grant
- rd admit <item-id> posts work:role-grant for the requester + triggers admission via convention server; work item closes on success
- rd admit <item-id> --role agent → grants agent role
- rd admit <item-id> --deny "reason" → denies the request (work item closes)
- rd revoke <pubkey-or-name> → posts work:role-grant role=revoked
- rd revoke <pubkey-or-name> --retroactive → posts retroactive revocation for every member that key admitted
- DoS defense: work:join-request rate-limited per source pubkey at convention server (10/h default, configurable)
- Test: full round-trip: rd join → rd admit → second user can run rd list on project

## Files (§10 Wave 3)
- cmd/rd/admit.go — new command (~80 LOC; avoid approve.go collision)
- cmd/rd/join.go — new command (~100 LOC)
- cmd/rd/revoke.go — new command (~40 LOC)

## Design reference
Design campfire: 36284e31fe77a3ecea91162d7c8826adc74b7dd1296f89ae69db68fab48a71cc. See §4.1 (consult join policy), §4.3 (rd admit), §4.5 (revocation).
IMPORTANT: rd admit calls TWO things atomically: (1) posts work:role-grant, (2) triggers convention server admission (which calls SDK join-grant). If the second step fails, the role-grant must be rolled back.

## Campfire protocol
- On start: cf <SWARM_CF> claim-item --item_id <this-item-id> --description "implementing Wave 3 rd join/admit/revoke commands"

## Goal
When a member's role is revoked, items they have claimed (status=in_progress, claimed by their pubkey) automatically become claimable again by other members.

## Done condition
- state.go Derive(): on work:role-grant role=revoked, all items with status=in_progress claimed by the revoked pubkey flip to status=ready
- Other members see these items in rd ready
- Test: member claims 3 items; their key is revoked; rd ready for other members shows those 3 items
- Test: retroactive revocation (--retroactive) propagates to all items claimed by any member admitted by the revoked key

## Files (§10 Wave 3)
- pkg/state/state.go — stranded-item reclaim rule on role=revoked (~30 LOC)

## Design reference
Design campfire: 36284e31fe77a3ecea91162d7c8826adc74b7dd1296f89ae69db68fab48a71cc. See §4.5 revocation and TOCTOU. The reclaim rule is a state-derivation rule — no new messages needed. Retroactive key revocation (--retroactive) posts a cascade of work:role-grant role=revoked messages for every member the revoked key admitted.

## test-depth: feature
## agent-type: implementer
## model-tier: haiku

## Goal
State derivation honors convention:server-binding for consequential operations, accepts all messages when no binding is present (bypass mode).

## Architecture: two-pass Derive()
The current Derive() is a single-pass message replay. Role authorization is Class 1 (deterministic from messages). Implement as a two-pass approach:

Pass 1 — build role map: scan all messages once for work:role-grant tags. Build map[senderPubkey]roleInfo. Latest role-grant for a pubkey wins. role='revoked' removes from map.

Pass 2 — existing replay: process operations as before, but add fulfillment gating for consequential ops (work:close, work:delegate, work:gate-resolve):
  - Find the active server-binding message (convention:server-binding tag, most recent valid_from <= now)
  - If no binding exists in the log → bypass mode: accept all (Wave 1 behavior)
  - If binding exists: look for a 'fulfills' message from the bound server_pubkey, OR a valid capability token in the operation payload
  - Pre-binding items (timestamp before first binding valid_from) → implicitly authorized
  - If neither fulfillment nor token nor bypass → operation rejected (dropped from derived state)

DO NOT add a projection store parameter to Derive(). Build the role map from messages inline. This preserves the pure function contract and is consistent with the append-only replay model.

## Done condition
- Test: operation with valid server fulfillment → accepted
- Test: operation with valid capability token → accepted offline
- Test: operation with no fulfillment + no token + no binding → accepted (bypass mode)
- Test: operation with no fulfillment + no token + binding present → rejected
- Test: pre-binding item (no binding at timestamp) → accepted
- All tests use real campfire message slices — no mocked state.

## Files (§10 Wave 2)
- pkg/state/state.go:245 Derive() — ~80-120 LOC added to the existing function
- Read pkg/state/state.go fully before modifying — understand existing msg loop structure (switch on hasTag)

## Design reference
Design campfire: 36284e31fe77a3ecea91162d7c8826adc74b7dd1296f89ae69db68fab48a71cc. See §3.1 server-binding, §3.2 bypass mode, §3.5 capability tokens, §3.4 member-roles (Class 1 = derivable from log directly).
Bypass mode is MANDATORY — do not merge without it.

## test-depth: feature
## agent-type: implementer
## model-tier: sonnet

## Goal
When a user runs rd join and the campfire's consult policy fires, the convention server materializes a work:join-request item visible in the maintainer's rd ready queue.

## Architecture: how SDK consult policy works
The consult join policy is in pkg/naming/join_policy.go. When a campfire has Policy='consult', the SDK posts the join request as a message to the ConsultCampfire (a second campfire ID). The convention server must:
1. Create or use a dedicated 'maintainer inbox' campfire as the ConsultCampfire
2. Write join-policy.json (at cfHome/join-policy.json) pointing ConsultCampfire to that inbox campfire ID
3. Join the inbox campfire as a member
4. Watch the inbox campfire for messages tagged with the join-request convention
5. For each join-request message, materialize a work:join-request item in the project campfire

The convention server does NOT 'run' the callback as a function. The SDK posts to the ConsultCampfire ID; the convention server watches that campfire. This is campfire-to-campfire messaging, not a function hook.

## test-depth: feature
## agent-type: implementer
## model-tier: sonnet

## Goal
A goroutine-based in-process convention server inside rd handles the solo authorization path: issues fulfillments, posts a server-binding on first use, issues capability tokens.

## How convention.Server works (read first)
pkg/convention/server.go — convention.NewServer(client, decl) creates a server for one declaration. RegisterHandler(opName, fn) registers a handler per operation. Serve(ctx, campfireID) blocks, polling for messages. Run one goroutine per operation you want to handle.

The server enforces min_operator_level automatically when WithProvenance is set. For the in-process solo server, use a ProvenanceChecker that always returns level 1 (self-asserted) since the user is local.

## Authorization matrix (§3.6) — embed this in the server
| Operation          | min_operator_level |
|--------------------|-------------------|
| work:create        | 1                 |
| work:claim         | 1                 |
| work:update        | 1                 |
| work:close         | 2                 |
| work:delegate      | 2                 |
| work:gate-resolve  | 2                 |
| work:role-grant    | 2                 |

Level 2 operations require a fulfillment from the convention server itself. The in-process server handles this by: receiving the operation, authorizing it (role check + level check), then posting a fulfills message back to the campfire.

## Done condition
- pkg/conventionserver/ package exists: authorization matrix, capability token issuance, server-binding bootstrap (~200 LOC)
- Server starts as a goroutine; posts convention:server-binding on first use (naming itself via its own pubkey)
- Server signs fulfillments with the rd process identity key (ephemeral — tied to rd process lifetime)
- member-roles named filter projection declared at server startup: predicate=(tag "work:role-grant"), entity_key=payload.pubkey, refresh=on-write — use store.UpsertProjectionEntry to maintain it on each work:role-grant message the server sees
- cmd/rd/root.go requireClient() boots the in-process server when no convention:server-binding exists yet in the campfire (first run) or when one exists naming this process's pubkey
- Test: rd close item-id in solo mode → fulfillment message appears in campfire log, signed by local identity key
- Test: server-binding message appears in campfire log on first close operation

payload.context: In cmd/rd/join.go, the beacon root is pinned to config (rdconfig.Save) BEFORE client.Join() is called. If the join fails (wrong campfire ID, transport error), the beacon root is permanently pinned to an untrusted value. The user must then explicitly run 'rd join --reset-beacon-root' to recover. Fix: move TOFU pin save to AFTER a successful join, or roll back on join failure. Severity: major.
payload.title: fix: TOFU beacon root pinned before join succeeds — spurious pin on failed join

payload.context: In cmd/rd/join.go, when stdin is not a terminal (CI pipelines, agent invocations), the TOFU first-use guard auto-pins any supplied --root value without user confirmation. A script that invokes 'rd join --root <attacker-controlled-id>' will silently pin a malicious beacon root. Subsequent joins using the correct root require --confirm or --reset-beacon-root to recover. Fix options: (1) require explicit --confirm flag even in non-interactive mode for first TOFU pin, (2) refuse to auto-pin in non-interactive mode and exit with a clear error, (3) document clearly that --confirm is required in CI. Severity: major — the whole point of TOFU is to surface first-use so users can verify the root.
payload.title: fix: TOFU non-interactive auto-pin silently accepts any beacon root in CI/agent context

payload.context: In cmd/rd/join.go + pkg/rdconfig/config.go, there is no file lock between Load() and Save(). Two concurrent 'rd join --root X' and 'rd join --root Y' processes can both read BeaconRoot='' simultaneously, both believe they are performing the first TOFU pin, and both write their respective roots — last writer wins silently. The expected behavior is that the second concurrent first-use should see a non-empty BeaconRoot and trigger the deviation guard. Fix: use file locking (e.g. flock or advisory lock on rd.json) around the Load-check-Save sequence in join, OR use an atomic compare-and-swap via OS-level rename semantics. Severity: minor in practice (concurrent rd join is rare) but is a logical integrity hole in the TOFU protocol.
payload.title: fix: TOCTOU race on TOFU beacon root pin — concurrent rd join can silently overwrite

payload.context: In pkg/state/state.go Derive(), when a cross-campfire ref appears as the BLOCKED item (p.BlockedID is cross-campfire, p.BlockerID is local), the code correctly attaches the warning to the local item (localItemID = p.BlockerID). However the warning message always uses p.BlockerID as the embedded ref string, not the actual cross-campfire ref (p.BlockedID). Result: the CrossCampfireWarning for this case says 'unresolved cross-campfire dep: <local-item-id> ...' — which is a local ID, not a cross-campfire ref. When crossdep.ResolveDeps processes this warning, extractRef returns the local item ID, ParseCrossCampfireRef returns nil (no dots), and the dep is returned as unresolvable with the wrong ref. This produces confusing output and means this dep relationship is never resolvable even when the user is a member of the relevant campfire. Fix: use p.BlockedID in the warning format string when blockedIsCross=true. Severity: minor (incorrect warning/display, not a security vulnerability).
payload.title: fix: cross-campfire warning message uses wrong item ID when blockedIsCross=true

payload.context: In pkg/crossdep/crossdep.go resolveSingle(), when full alias lookup on 'acme.frontend' fails, the code retries with only the last segment 'frontend'. If the user has a short alias 'frontend' pointing to a different campfire than the one the cross-campfire ref author intended, the dep resolves against the wrong campfire. This means a crafted work:block message with 'evil.frontend.item-abc' will resolve against whatever campfire the user has aliased as 'frontend', not 'evil.frontend'. An attacker who can send messages to a shared campfire and knows the user's alias names can probe which aliases exist (an alias hit vs miss produces different display output). The fallback was added for usability but weakens the namespace isolation that the dot-separated campfire name path is meant to provide. Fix: remove the last-segment fallback, or at minimum add a warning when the fallback is used. Severity: major — breaks namespace isolation guarantees.
payload.title: fix: last-segment alias fallback in crossdep may resolve unintended campfire

payload.context: In cmd/rd/join.go, neither campfireID (args[0]) nor beaconRoot (--root flag) are validated before use. Both are stored directly to config and passed to client.Join(). While the current uses are comparison and truncated display (no injection risk), storing arbitrary strings as campfire IDs in the config creates risk for future code that might use them in paths or protocol operations. Recommended: validate that both values match the expected hex ID format (64 hex characters) before proceeding. Severity: minor — no exploitable injection currently, but hygiene issue.
payload.title: fix: no input validation on campfireID or beaconRoot in rd join

payload.reason: Duplicate — superseded by ready-f34 which has the correct title for the captoken IssueToken panic bug

payload.context: pkg/captoken/captoken.go:188 — IssueToken() accesses operations[0] at line 188 before the len(operations)==0 guard at line 189. If called with an empty slice, this panics with index out of range. The len guard must be checked first. Trigger: any caller passing an empty operations list to IssueToken(). This is a latent panic in the capability token issuance path.

The serverBindingPayload struct is defined twice with incompatible field types: (1) conventionserver/server.go:410 — ValidFrom int64, ValidUntil int64 (writes JSON numbers). (2) state/state.go:206 — ValidFrom string, ValidUntil string (tries to unmarshal into strings). JSON numbers do NOT unmarshal into Go string fields — the field silently stays empty string. parseTimestamp('') returns 0. Result: findActiveServerBinding always returns nil because validFrom==0 causes every binding to be skipped as 'not yet valid'. This breaks isOperationAuthorized for ALL consequential operations (close, delegate, gate-resolve) in a campfire that has a server-binding declared — they are all rejected as unauthorized. Observable failure: work:close messages are silently dropped from derived state when a server-binding exists.

cmd/rd/dep.go:264 — buildDepTree() sets visited[id]=true (line 248), processes children (recursively calling buildDepTree with same visited map), then calls delete(visited, id) at line 264. The delete is meant to allow the same node to appear in multiple branches (not truly a cycle). However, if there IS a genuine cycle (A blocks B, B blocks A), the delete means: when processing A we mark visited[A]=true, descend into B, descend back into A — A IS in visited so we return the cycle node. But after B finishes and we delete visited[B], if A appears again via a different path, we'd recurse again. In a true cycle A->B->A, the delete at the end of A's processing means if A is reached via a third path, visited[A] is gone and we recurse again — infinite recursion/stack overflow. Trigger: item with cyclic block edges (A blocks B, B blocks A) plus a third path to A.

payload.reason: Dead code sweep complete: 32 unused items found (6 from staticcheck, 26 unreachable functions). All items identified as safe-to-delete. See item notes for full list.

payload.context: cmd/rd/join.go:278 — validateNameFormat() checks for path traversal via strings.Contains(input, '../') and input=='..'. But it misses the trailing case: 'foo/..' passes validation because it doesn't contain '../' (no trailing slash) and is not exactly '..'. An attacker or buggy name resolution returning 'malicious/..' would pass the format check. The fix: also check strings.HasSuffix(input, '/..'). Severity: medium — defense in depth issue since the validated value is used as a campfire name argument, not a filesystem path directly. But validateNameFormat is designed as a security gate, and having a gap undermines its purpose.

cmd/rd/admit.go:222,233 and cmd/rd/revoke.go:78,117 — multiple sites truncate message IDs or pubkeys with [:12] without a length guard. In admit.go lines 222 and 233: grantMsgID[:12] is called on the message ID returned by admitThenGrant. If the executor returns an ID shorter than 12 chars (unusual but possible in test doubles, error paths, or future protocol changes), this panics. Same pattern in revoke.go:78 result.MessageID[:12] and line 117 r2.MessageID[:12]. The display-only truncations for displayKey are guarded by a prior length check, but the grantMsgID/result.MessageID truncations are not. Fix: use min(12, len(id)) or check len before slicing, consistent with the minInt helper already defined in send.go.

payload.title: BUG: role-grant granted_at sent as RFC3339 string but Derive() parses as int64 nanoseconds — field silently ignored

payload.context: cmd/rd/admit.go:274,278 and cmd/rd/revoke.go:63,68 — granted_at is built as RFC3339 string: now := time.Now().UTC().Format(time.RFC3339). It is passed in the role-grant payload as a string. In state/state.go:473, Derive() parses role-grant messages into a struct with 'GrantedAt int64'. JSON unmarshal of a string value into int64 fails silently in Go — the field stays 0. So p.GrantedAt==0 always, and the code falls back to m.Timestamp (message arrival time). The grantedAt ordering comparison on line 488 ('grantedAt >= existing.grantedAt') thus compares message timestamps, not the explicitly provided granted_at. This is always correct in practice (messages are replayed in order), but the granted_at field intended for migration/import purposes is completely non-functional. Fix: use int64 unix nanoseconds in the payload, or parse RFC3339 in the struct.

payload.title: BUG: isHexString in inbox_watcher rejects uppercase hex pubkeys — valid join requests silently dropped

payload.context: pkg/conventionserver/inbox_watcher.go:217 — isHexString() only accepts lowercase hex [0-9a-f]. But join-request senders may encode their pubkey with uppercase hex [0-9A-F] (e.g., Go's encoding/hex produces lowercase, but other implementations may not). A valid 64-char pubkey with any uppercase character is rejected at line 121-123 with 'invalid pubkey format'. By contrast, isHex() in cmd/rd/join.go:410 accepts both cases. Fix: accept both cases in isHexString, or use strings.ToLower before validation.
